text:
2017 BCSC 259 R. v. Topham
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF BRITISH COLUMBIA
Citation:
R. v. Topham,
2017 BCSC 259
Date: 20170217
Docket: 25166
Registry: Quesnel
Regina
v.
Roy Arthur Topham
Before: The Honourable Mr. Justice Butler
Ruling on Bedford Threshold Issue
Counsel for the Crown:
Rodney G. Garson
Counsel for the Accused:
Barclay W. Johnson
Place and Date of Trial/Hearing:
Quesnel, B.C.
October 3-6, 2016
Vancouver, B.C.
October 19, 2016
Victoria, B.C.
November 8 and 9, 2016
Place and Date of Judgment:
Quesnel, B.C.
February 17, 2017
[1] On November 12, 2015, following a 14-day trial, Arthur Topham was convicted by a jury of one count of willfully promoting hatred against an identifiable group, people of the Jewish religion or ethnic origin, contrary to s. 319(2) of the Criminal Code. Prior to the trial, he brought an application pursuant to the Constitutional Question Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 68, challenging the constitutionality of s. 319(2). I heard that application and in reasons indexed as R. v. Topham, 2015 BCSC 1185, ruled that the hearing of the application should take place following the trial, in the event of a conviction. Part of the rationale for that decision was that if Mr. Topham was convicted, there would be a factual foundation for the constitutional challenge. He has now renewed the application.
[2] In R. v. Keegstra, [1990] 3 S.C.R. 697, the Supreme Court of Canada considered the constitutional validity of s. 319(2) of the Criminal Code. The Court found that the section infringed the right to freedom of expression guaranteed by s. 2(b) of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Part I of the Constitution Act, 1982 being Schedule B to the Canada Act 1982 (U.K.), 1982 c. 11 (the "Charter"). However, the majority also concluded that the Criminal Code provision was saved by s. 1 of the Charter as a reasonable limit that was demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society.
[3] Given the doctrine that lower courts must follow the decisions of higher courts, Keegstra is binding on this Court. However, as the Supreme Court of Canada explained in Canada (Attorney General) v. Bedford, 2013 SCC 72, at para. 42, there are circumstances in which a lower court can depart from a binding statement of law. That can be done in only two situations: 1) where a new legal issue is raised; and 2) where there "is a change in circumstances or evidence that fundamentally shifts the parameters of the debate".
[4] The defence says that this Court does not have to follow Keegstra, as both situations which allow for departure from a binding precedent exist in this case. Further, the defence says that the s. 1 determination in Keegstra is not binding on this Court. The Crown takes the position that Keegstra is binding and that neither situation which would allow for reconsideration of that ruling is present.
[5] In light of Bedford, and in order to resolve the dispute over the application of Keegstra, the parties agreed that Mr. Topham's application should be considered in two stages. At the first stage, the Court would consider whether the Bedford threshold for reconsidering the Keegstra ruling has been met. In a hearing which lasted seven days, Mr. Topham presented evidence which he says is relevant to reconsideration of the ruling and the parties advanced argument on the threshold issue.
[6] This is my decision on the threshold issue. For the reasons that follow, I conclude that this case does not meet the threshold necessary to reconsider Keegstra and Mr. Topham's application challenging the constitutionality of s. 319(2) is dismissed. Accordingly, there is no need for the second stage of the application. For clarity, and in the event this decision is overturned on appeal, I note that the parties contemplated and I agreed that at the second stage, the Crown would be permitted to present evidence relevant to the s. 1 issue: whether s. 319(2) of the Criminal Code is a reasonable limitation on s. 2(b) of the Charter. Of course, at the second stage the parties would also have the opportunity to present argument on the constitutional issues, including on the question of an appropriate remedy under s. 52 of the Constitution Act, 1982.
The Bedford Threshold
[7] The threshold that must be met before a lower court can reconsider a binding authority is set out in Bedford. In that case, lower courts were asked to reconsider the decision in Reference re ss. 193 and 195.1(1)(c) of the Criminal Code (Man.), [1990] 1 S.C.R. 1123. The Court agreed that lower courts may, in limited circumstances, revisit conclusions reached in binding decisions of the Supreme Court of Canada. That is the case even though, "[c]ertainty in the law requires that courts follow and apply authoritative precedents" and that is "the foundational principle upon which the common law relies."
[8] In Bedford at para. 42, McLachlin C.J., delivering the judgment for the Court, succinctly explained the circumstances in which a lower court can revisit binding conclusions:
In my view, a trial judge can consider and decide arguments based on Charter provisions that were not raised in the earlier case; this constitutes a new legal issue. Similarly, the matter may be revisited if new legal issues are raised as a consequence of significant developments in the law, or if there is a change in the circumstances or evidence that fundamentally shifts the parameters of the debate.
[9] The threshold was established to balance "the need for finality and stability with the recognition that when an appropriate case arises for revisiting precedent, a lower court must be able to perform its full role." At the same time, the Court stressed at para. 44, that the "threshold for revisiting a matter is not an easy one to reach."
[10] The Supreme Court of Canada reaffirmed the Bedford threshold in Carter v. Canada (Attorney General), 2015 SCC 5. It reiterated the two situations in which a settled ruling may be considered, and approved of the trial court judge's decision to revisit at para. 45:
Both conditions were met in this case. The trial judge explained her decision to revisit Rodriguez by noting the changes in both the legal framework for s. 7 and the evidence on controlling the risk of abuse associated with assisted suicide.
[11] Before turning to the defence arguments for revisiting Keegstra, it is necessary to dispense with the alternative argument that the decision in Keegstra is not binding on this Court. The defence advanced that argument at the pre-trial application and has renewed it on this application. It is expressed in the written submissions as follows:
The Supreme Court's 4-3 decision in Keegstra is not, and was not intended to be, a blanket endorsement of Section 319(2) in all circumstances, but rather a specific statement that it was a reasonable limit on freedom of expression in the circumstances of that case.
[12] The defence argues that in this prosecution, and indeed in every prosecution under s. 319(2), the Crown is obliged to present sufficient evidence to satisfy the s. 1 justification to take into account the particular circumstances of the case. In other words, the defence asserts that the s. 1 justification in Keegstra was limited to the facts of that case.
[13] As the Crown argues, this submission is based on a fundamental misunderstanding of the doctrine of stare decisis in Charter cases. When the Supreme Court of Canada concludes that a legislative provision violates the Charter but is saved by s. 1, the effect of such a ruling is that the legislation is constitutional and requires no further justification for that violation. If the defence argument is accepted, it would mean that any s. 1 justification would have no binding effect except in cases with identical adjudicative facts. Such an approach would undermine the common law principle of stare decisis in Charter cases that turn on the application of s. 1.
[14] The defence relies on a number of authorities in support of this proposition. First it says that the decision in R. v. Hess; R. v. Nguyen, [1990] 2 S.C.R. 906, specifically requires courts to consider s. 1 on a case-by-case basis. It refers to the majority's statement at 926:
But section 1 of the Charter is not itself devoid of values. It stipulates that the impugned provisions must be "demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society". This term cannot be read in such a way as to give licence to governments to infringe rights in any way they please. The values of a free and democratic society must be respected. Whether or not they have been respected must be decided on a case by case basis.
[15] That statement undoubtedly governs the proper approach to justifying Charter breaches under s. 1. However, there is nothing in the decision which suggests that once legislation has been found to be constitutional, the s. 1 justification does not bind lower courts in subsequent cases dealing with the same statutory provision.
[16] The defence also relies on Greater Vancouver Regional District Employees' Union v. Greater Vancouver Regional District, 2001 BCCA 435, for the proposition that a case-by-case analysis must be undertaken. In that case, the majority rejected the proposition that the decision in McKinney v. University of Guelph, [1990] 3 S.C.R. 229, stood for the principle that all mandatory retirement policies in the public sector that do not violate human rights legislation are saved by s. 1 of the Charter. The decision in Greater Vancouver Regional District does not assist the defence position. The court was not being asked to reconsider legislation that had been found to be constitutional; it was being asked to apply a prior decision about an Ontario government retirement policy to a different retirement policy in British Columbia.
[17] The defence also relies on the decision in Dagenais v. Canadian Broadcasting Corp., [1994] 3 S.C.R. 835. Once again, that decision has no application to the present case. The Court was not considering the constitutionality of legislation, let alone revisiting an earlier ruling on constitutionality. It considered the validity of a publication ban imposed under a common law discretionary rule and held, unsurprisingly, that the party seeking to justify the limitation of a right bears the burden of justifying the limitation.
[18] The defence position is based on a misconception about what circumstances might warrant a lower court revisiting a constitutional ruling. The defence suggests that if the underlying facts supporting the conviction are different from those in the prior ruling, the s. 1 justification ruling can be revisited. That is not the kind of change in circumstances which the Bedford threshold contemplates. The "circumstances or evidence" that must change significantly to warrant re-visitation are related to the legal and evidentiary record underpinning the higher court's prior conclusion regarding the Charter violation and the s. 1 justification. This is clear from the decision in R. v Oakes, [1986] 1 S.C.R. 103, which describes the nature of the inquiry that must be undertaken when examining a s. 1 justification.
[19] The comments in Bedford at para. 46 recognize how difficult it is to reach the reconsideration threshold and that, when dealing with a s. 2(b) violation, it is not sufficient to simply re-characterize the type or nature of expression said to be infringed:
These considerations do not apply to the question of whether the communication provision is a justified limit on freedom of expression. That issue was decided in the Prostitution Reference. Re-characterizing the type of expression alleged to be infringed did not convert this argument into a new legal issue, nor did the more current evidentiary record or the shift in attitudes and perspectives amount to a change in the circumstances or evidence that fundamentally shifted the parameters of the debate.
[20] Justice Doherty made a similar point speaking for the majority in the Ontario Court of Appeal decision in Canada (Attorney General) v. Bedford, 2012 ONCA 186 at para. 83:
In our view, the need for a robust application of stare decisis is particularly important in the context of Charter litigation. Given the nature of the s. 1 test, especially in controversial matters, the evidence and legislative facts will continue to evolve, as will values, attitudes and perspectives. But this evolution alone is not sufficient to trigger a reconsideration in the lower courts.
[21] In summary, the Bedford threshold is met where the court is satisfied of one of the following:
a) A new legal issue is raised. This can occur either because of significant developments in the law or because a new argument is made based on Charter provisions that were not raised in the earlier case; or
b) There is a change in the circumstances or evidence that fundamentally shifts the parameters of the debate. This inquiry focuses on the legal and evidentiary record underpinning the higher court's prior conclusion on the Charter violation and s. 1 justification. The changing circumstances or evidence may also relate to changes in society's values, attitudes and perspectives since the time of the earlier ruling.
The Decision in Keegstra
[22] In Keegstra, it was evident that s. 319(2) infringed s. 2(b) of the Charter. Accordingly, the central issue was whether the infringement was a reasonable limitation that is demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society. Chief Justice Dickson wrote the majority decision. He began his analysis by quoting from his decision in Oakes to emphasize the breadth of the ideals and concepts that inform a s. 1 consideration, at 736:
The Court must be guided by the values and principles essential to a free and democratic society which I believe embody, to name but a few, respect for the inherent dignity of the human person, commitment to social justice and equality, accommodation of a wide variety of beliefs, respect for cultural and group identity, and faith in social and political institutions which enhance the participation of individuals and groups in society. The underlying values and principles of a free and democratic society are the genesis of the rights and freedoms guaranteed by the Charter and the ultimate standard against which a limit on a right or freedom must be shown, despite its effect, to be reasonable and demonstrably justified.
[23] Proceeding with the analysis, Dickson C.J. considered the objective of s. 319(2). In doing so, he considered the 1966 Report of the Special Committee on Hate Propaganda in Canada, commonly known as the Cohen Committee, which found that the incidence of hate propaganda was not insignificant and that it was harmful. Dickson C.J. also concluded that the presence of hate propaganda in Canada was substantial enough to warrant concern and that it caused two sorts of injury. First, "there is harm done to members of the target group. It is indisputable that the emotional damage caused by words may be of grave psychological and social consequence." (at 746). A second, "harmful effect of hate propaganda which is of pressing and substantial concern is its influence upon society at large . It is thus not inconceivable that the active dissemination of hate propaganda can attract individuals to its cause, and in the process create serious discord between various cultural groups in society." (at 747).
[24] In addition to examining the Cohen Committee report, he considered a number of subsequent reports that echoed the findings and concerns expressed in that report. In addition to the various reports, Dickson C.J. reviewed international human rights principles and the international obligations assumed by Canada. At 750, he considered how those informed the s. 1 analysis:
Generally speaking, the international human rights obligations taken on by Canada reflect the values and principles of a free and democratic society, and thus those values and principles that underlie the Charter itself (Reference re Public Service Employee Relations Act (Alta.), [1987] 1 S.C.R. 313, per Dickson C.J., at p. 348). Moreover, international human rights law and Canada's commitments in that area are of particular significance in assessing the importance of Parliament's objective under s. 1. As stated in Slaight Communications Inc. v. Davidson, supra, at pp. 1056-57:
Canada's international human rights obligations should inform not only the interpretation of the content of the rights guaranteed by the Charter but also the interpretation of what can constitute pressing and substantial s. 1 objectives which may justify restrictions upon those rights.
In the context of justifying an infringement of s. 2(b), the majority in Slaight made a point of noting that a value enjoying status as an international human right is generally to be ascribed a high degree of importance under s. 1 of the Charter (pp. 1056-57).
[25] Chief Justice Dickson then reviewed the international human rights instruments to which Canada adhered, including the International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination ("CERD") and the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights ("ICCPR"). At 754-55, he acknowledged the existence of international debate regarding the balance between freedom of expression and the suppression of hate propaganda, and explained why Canada's adherence to these international instruments is important to the s. 1 analysis:
But despite debate Canada, along with other members of the international community, has indicated a commitment to prohibiting hate propaganda, and in my opinion this Court must have regard to that commitment in investigating the nature of the government objective behind s. 319(2) of the Criminal Code. That the international community has collectively acted to condemn hate propaganda, and to oblige State Parties to CERD and ICCPR to prohibit such expression, thus emphasizes the importance of the objective behind s. 319(2) and the principles of equality and the inherent dignity of all persons that infuse both international human rights and the Charter.
[26] The analysis of the Chief Justice then turned to other provisions of the Charter, including s. 15 and s. 27, which he found supported the objectives behind s. 319(2). Considering s. 15, he noted at 756:
In light of the Charter commitment to equality, and the reflection of this commitment in the framework of s. 1, the objective of the impugned legislation is enhanced insofar as it seeks to ensure the equality of all individuals in Canadian society. The message of the expressive activity covered by s. 319(2) is that members of identifiable groups are not to be given equal standing in society, and are not human beings equally deserving of concern, respect and consideration. The harms caused by this message run directly counter to the values central to a free and democratic society, and in restricting the promotion of hatred Parliament is therefore seeking to bolster the notion of mutual respect necessary in a nation which venerates the equality of all persons.
[27] With regard to s. 27, he observed at 757 that:
The value expressed in s. 27 cannot be casually dismissed in assessing the validity of s. 319(2) under s. 1, and I am of the belief that s. 27 and the commitment to a multicultural vision of our nation bear notice in emphasizing the acute importance of the objective of eradicating hate propaganda from society.
[28] Having identified the types of harm flowing from hate speech and examined the values and principles essential to a free and democratic society, Dickson C.J. set out the conclusion of the majority at 758:
In my opinion, it would be impossible to deny that Parliament's objective in enacting s. 319(2) is of the utmost importance. Parliament has recognized the substantial harm that can flow from hate propaganda, and in trying to prevent the pain suffered by target group members and to reduce racial, ethnic and religious tension in Canada has decided to suppress the wilful promotion of hatred against identifiable groups. The nature of Parliament's objective is supported not only by the work of numerous study groups, but also by our collective historical knowledge of the potentially catastrophic effects of the promotion of hatred (Jones, supra, per La Forest J., at pp. 299-300). Additionally, the international commitment to eradicate hate propaganda and the stress placed upon equality and multiculturalism in the Charter strongly buttress the importance of this objective. I consequently find that the first part of the test under s. 1 of the Charter is easily satisfied and that a powerfully convincing legislative objective exists such as to justify some limit on freedom of expression.
[29] The defence has made much of the fact that the Court was split in Keegstra and that three justices dissented. However, it is worthwhile to note that the minority agreed with the majority conclusion as to the first part of the Oakes test; the importance of the legislative objective of addressing the serious harm posed by hate speech: at 812. The real debate in Keegstra was whether the means chosen to further the objective are proportional to the ends.
[30] The Chief Justice commenced the proportionality analysis by considering the relationship between the prohibited expression and the s. 2(b) right. He reiterated his conclusion that the harm caused by the hate propaganda, "is deeply offensive, hurtful and damaging to target group members, misleading to his listeners, and antithetical to the furtherance of tolerance and understanding in society." (at 761-62). He also emphasized that the expression prohibited by s. 319(2) is not closely linked to the rationale underlying s. 2(b) which means that the expression is "of limited importance when measured against free expression values." (at 762).
[31] The Chief Justice had no difficulty in finding a rational connection between the criminal prohibition of hate propaganda and the Parliamentary objectives of protecting target group members from that kind of expression and fostering harmonious social relations within communities in Canada: at 767. He concludes that these purposes are valid and that the means chosen is rational in both theory and operation.
[32] The Chief Justice then considered whether s. 319(2) minimally impaired the Charter guarantee of freedom of expression. In concluding that it was a minimal impairment, he focused on the demanding mens rea element of the offence. He accepted the interpretation of "wilfully" from R. v. Buzzanga and Durocher (1979), 49 C.C.C. (2d) 369 (Ont. C.A.): the Crown must prove that an accused subjectively desires the promotion of hatred or foresees such a consequence as being certain or substantially certain. The Crown burden of proving subjective intent beyond a reasonable doubt is difficult to meet. This serves to minimize the impairment of freedom of expression: at 775.
[33] The analysis of the mens rea also included the observation that the word "promotes" requires the Crown to prove that the accused actively supports or instigates hatred. In addition, the use of the term "hatred" increases the burden on the Crown because it must prove that the accused intended to promote hatred, "a most extreme emotion that belies reason; an emotion that, if exercised against members of an identifiable group, implies that those individuals are to be despised, scorned, denied respect and made subject to ill-treatment on the basis of group affiliation." (at 777)
[34] Chief Justice Dickson concluded his analysis at 785-86:
To summarize the above discussion, in light of the great importance of Parliament's objective and the discounted value of the expression at issue I find that the terms of s. 319(2) create a narrowly confined offence which suffers from neither overbreadth nor vagueness. This interpretation stems largely from my view that the provision possesses a stringent mens rea requirement, necessitating either an intent to promote hatred or knowledge of the substantial certainty of such, and is also strongly supported by the conclusion that the meaning of the word "hatred" is restricted to the most severe and deeply-felt form of opprobrium. Additionally, however, the conclusion that s. 319(2) represents a minimal impairment of the freedom of expression gains credence through the exclusion of private conversation from its scope, the need for the promotion of hatred to focus upon an identifiable group and the presence of the s. 319(3) defences.
Position of Mr. Topham
[35] The defence argues that it has raised a new legal issue. It says Keegstra concerned communications which were forced on students by a teacher. Those circumstances are very different from the present case. Mr. Topham operated a website, Radical Press, and his communications were imposed on no one, but were available to those interested in the subject matter. Further, the materials which he republished are lawfully available to Canadians from dozens of other websites including mainstream sites such as Amazon. In these circumstances, the defence argues that the Court in Keegstra drew the objective behind s. 319(2) too broadly. In the internet age, the defence argues that the same materials and viewpoints which Mr. Topham communicated are readily available for consumption from many sources; these circumstances raise a new legal issue in the s. 1 analysis as to the breadth of the objective. The defence argues that the effect of upholding the conviction would be to criminalize all offensive communications.
[36] The defence says the new legal issue has arisen in part because of the broad dissemination of materials and viewpoints on the Internet that is similar to Mr. Topham's but also because of developments in the law. In Saskatchewan (Human Rights Commission) v. Whatcott, 2013 SCC 11 [Whatcott], the Court held that laws against hate speech can only be justified where the expression in question "has the potential to lead to discrimination and other harmful effects". Further, in RJR-MacDonald Inc. v. Canada (Attorney General), [1995] 3 S.C.R. 199, the Court struck down the prohibition on tobacco advertising in Canadian magazines because it was not rationally connected to the objective of the legislation. Of particular significance was the fact that the law would have been ineffective because 65% of magazines in the Canadian market were imported and would not have been affected by the impugned legislation. The defence says the circumstances are similar here; the material Mr. Topham republishes is widely available from other sources and so the objective of the prohibition cannot be fulfilled.
[37] The defence argues that these developments in the law, when applied to the circumstances of the present case, raise a new legal issue at the second stage of the Oakes analysis; the finding in Keegstra - that s. 319(2) had effects proportional to its objective - no longer applies because of the widespread availability of similar, potentially hateful material.
[38] The defence also argues there has been a change in circumstance that fundamentally shifts the parameters of the debate. It relies on the expert opinion of Dr. Michael Persinger for the proposition that the Cohen Committee findings are based on archaic psychological theories which have been outdated for decades. Dr. Persinger says there is no direct experimental evidence to establish that people subjected to racial or religious hatred may suffer psychological stress or loss of self-esteem. The defence says this is significant because the majority in Keegstra accepted that persons belonging to racial or religious groups targeted by hate speech would be humiliated and degraded. Further, the defence argues, the Court in Whatcott specifically found that human rights legislation prohibiting expression that "ridicules, belittles or otherwise affronts the dignity" of members of a protected group was not rationally connected to the legislative purpose.
[39] In written submissions, the defence summed up its position as follows:
A case of hate speech by a schoolteacher in the 1980s, prior to the widespread availability of the internet, cannot automatically be determinative of a Charter application involving internet publication of material which is legal to possess and lawfully available at the click of a button. Advancements in science and technology which have occurred since Keegstra represent a change of circumstances, fundamentally shifting the parameters of the debate.
Position of the Crown
[40] The Crown says the Chief Justice's analysis of the s. 1 justification of s. 319(2) in Keegstra fully explored the rich and multifaceted debate about the restriction that the hate speech prohibitions place on freedom of expression. It involved wide-ranging legal considerations informed by an amalgam of historical, psychological, sociological and philosophical concepts, as well as observations of common sense arising from human and judicial experience. The Crown says the defence has failed to show that there is any basis on which to revisit that s. 1 analysis.
[41] The Crown emphasizes that Dr. Persinger's evidence was limited to narrow fields of cognitive psychology, clinical neuropsychology and behavioural neuroscience. He could not and did not opine in areas of history, and social psychology, sociology, philosophy or political theory, all of which shaped the debate that gave rise to the ruling in Keegstra. Given the breadth of the parameters of the debate and the limits of Dr. Persinger's evidence, the Crown says on that basis alone the defence has failed to meet the Bedford threshold.
[42] Further, the defence argument that rests on Dr. Persinger's opinion does not adequately challenge the Court's finding in Keegstra that hate speech harms targeted people. The majority concluded that hate speech gives rise to a reasonable apprehension of harm at three distinct levels: the individual, the social or cultural, and the political or philosophical. Dr. Persinger's opinion only deals with one aspect of the s. 1 debate - the psychological aspect of individual harm. It falls far short of raising a new issue for consideration.
[43] The Crown says that Dr. Persinger's criticism of Dr. Harry Kaufmann (who was referred to in the Cohen Committee report) should be afforded no weight. They argue that Dr. Persinger's opinions were vague and unsupported by explanation or analysis and amounted to bald assertions. Further, he did not directly challenge the conclusions of Dr. Kaufmann or the Cohen Committee. In addition, he acknowledged that there is evidence supporting the proposition that hate speech causes psychological and physical harm.
[44] The Crown says the defence assertion that it has raised a new legal issue is without merit. That argument is based on a false premise: that s. 319(2) bans republication of material that is already widely available. The argument fails to take into account the highly restricted nature and scope of the activity that the section criminalizes. It only restricts communications made other than in private conversation, and requires an elevated and specific mens rea: the Crown must prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the accused communicated statements with the intent to promote hatred against an identifiable group. It also fails to consider the defences afforded by way of s. 319(3).
[45] The argument also fails because it is based on the premise that Mr. Topham was convicted by the jury for merely republishing works written by others. This ignores the evidence before the jury which was sufficient to establish that Mr. Topham assembled the various links to other material, wrote the publication, Israel Must Perish, and authored numerous posts or commentaries on the website.
[46] In written submissions, the Crown summed up its position as follows:
Dickson C.J.'s reasons for judgment in Keegstra painstakingly and exhaustively identify, analyse and assess all relevant s. 1 arguments touching upon the pressing legislative objective of s. 319(2) of the Code, its rational connection to the objective, its minimal impairment of the s. 2(b) Charter right, and the balancing of its salutary and deleterious effects. The arguments for and against s. 1 justification are comprehensively addressed; the applicant raises no new legal issue. Redefining the legislative objective of s. 319(2) (by misconstruing its scope) for use as a springboard to arguing absence of a rational connection does not raise a new legal issue. It amounts only to disagreement with the Supreme Court of Canada's rulings in Keegstra and Whatcott.
Issues
[47] It is difficult to discern a distinct legal issue in the arguments advanced by the defence. In part, this is because the arguments, as the Crown notes, are based on an erroneous assumption about the underlying facts: the defence repeatedly asserts that Mr. Topham only republished materials written by others. This was not the case presented to the jury. Further, the defence submissions erroneously attribute to the Crown (and presumably to parliament) a variety of "objectives" for the impugned legislation which do not accurately reflect the ruling in Keegstra. The defence asserts that the Crown's apparent objective was "discouraging publication of offensive material" or "the removal of hate propaganda by any means", or "to render the various books on [Mr. Topham's] website unavailable to the Canadian public". Of course, the Crown's objective is irrelevant; it is Parliament's objective, as identified in Keegstra, which must be considered.
[48] While the defence argument fails to identify a distinct legal issue, I will consider the defence position by examining two issues which are in accord with the Court's articulation of the threshold in Bedford:
1. Is there a new legal issue regarding the s. 1 justification for s. 319(2) that was not considered in Keegstra?
2. Is there a change in circumstances or evidence that fundamentally shifts the parameters of the debate?
[49] Before considering these issues, I should note that the arguments were advanced on the assumption that neither party bears the onus on a Bedford threshold application. I agree this was an appropriate approach to take. The Court must be satisfied on the basis of the evidence and argument that the threshold has been met before reconsidering a binding authority. Imposing an onus adds little to that assessment. In practice, a court will likely resolve any doubt as to whether the threshold has been met by permitting the party challenging the existing authority to present evidence and full argument. Here, the defence was afforded a full opportunity to present evidence and argument on the threshold issue. As I will explain, I am left with no doubt that the threshold for reconsidering Keegstra has not been met.
Issue 1: Is there a new legal issue regarding the s. 1 justification for s. 319(2) that was not considered in Keegstra?
[50] Section 319(2) provides as follows:
(2) Every one who, by communicating statements, other than in private conversation, wilfully promotes hatred against any identifiable group is guilty of
(a) an indictable offence and is liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years; or
(b) an offence punishable on summary conviction.
[51] As I have noted, in Keegstra the majority found that the provision infringed the s. 2(b) right to freedom of expression but withstood the constitutional challenge by justification under s. 1 of the Charter. A new legal issue will necessarily be one that was not considered in Keegstra. The defence does not raise any Charter provision that was not considered by the Court in Keegstra. Accordingly, if there is a new legal issue, it must be because the previous Oakes analysis is no longer valid, or because there have been significant developments in the law.
[52] The two main thrusts of the defence argument on this issue are: 1) the wide availability of similar materials and viewpoints on the Internet creates a new situation and thus a new legal issue; and 2) Whatcott changed the Court's characterization of the legislative objective for s. 319(2) which requires revisiting the s. 1 analysis in Keegstra. I will consider these separately.
Internet availability of hate materials
[53] I understand this argument to be aimed at the rational connection between the object of the legislation and the means chosen to effect that object. In other words, the defence says that the prohibition created by s. 319(2) is no longer fair because the Internet allows easy access to the publications to which Mr. Topham provided links and to viewpoints similar to those he communicated. He argues that s. 319(2) does not minimally impair the right to freedom of expression; it takes away the right of individuals in Canada to share material and opinions that others distribute freely. In other words, he says the existence of the Internet as a means for communication has changed the legal analysis.
[54] The first thing to note about this argument is that it is based in part on the false assumption that Mr. Topham only republished material written by others. As I have noted, that was not the evidence before the jury. The links to other materials were assembled by him and he added commentary or posts regarding the various works to which he provided links. Further, he wrote or created Israel Must Perish and published it on his website. He maintained in argument that it was a satire but the jury did not accept that position. Nevertheless, to the extent that his argument is based on the proposition that the s. 1 analysis must be revisited to take into account that s. 319(2) criminalizes republication of material on the Internet, it cannot succeed. Quite simply, that was not the evidence before the Court and so the issue does not arise.
[55] The evidence presented by the defence on this issue included a series of screen shots of Google searches for some of the offensive publications including, The Protocols of the Learned Elders of Zion, The Biological Jew, The Jewish Religion: Its Influence Today and Germany Must Perish. Mr. Topham's website republished or provided links to these materials. The Google searches revealed a large number of hits for the publications or links to them. There is no doubt that at some of those links the publications are available to be read in whole or in part. There is no question that these materials are widely available for viewing or purchase on the Internet. The screen shots also showed that Mr. Topham's work, Israel Must Perish, returned "about 3,300 results" on a Google search.
[56] The widespread availability on the Internet of speech that could be characterized as hateful does not change the s. 1 legal analysis. Section 319(2) does not distinguish between methods of communication, although it does not criminalize statements communicated "in private conversation". The Internet facilitates wide public communication. That is why it is used in almost all endeavours in our society. Businesses, universities, governments, courts, as well as social, political, sporting and charitable organizations make public communications through the Internet. Of course, private individuals also make public communications by way of internet posts, blogs, and social media commentary. Some, like Mr. Topham, do so through their own websites.
[57] The fact that the Internet has significantly increased the ability of people, businesses and governments to communicate widely does not raise a new issue in the s. 1 justification of the Criminal Code provision. Indeed, the fact that the Internet facilitates the easy exchange of information with many people means it can cause the harm outlined in Keegstra. Indeed, the Internet may make the risk of harm associated with hate speech a more pressing issue. This was noted in Whatcott at para. 72 where Rothstein J., wrote for the Court:
The majority in Keegstra and Taylor reviewed evidence detailing the potential risks of harm from the dissemination of messages of hate, including the 1966 Report of the Special Committee on Hate Propaganda in Canada, commonly known as the Cohen Committee. The Cohen Committee wrote at a time when the experiences of fascism in Italy and National Socialism in Germany were in recent memory. Almost 50 years later, I cannot say that those examples have proven to be isolated and unrepeated at our current point in history. One need only look to the former Yugoslavia, Cambodia, Rwanda, Darfur, or Uganda to see more recent examples of attempted cleansing or genocide on the basis of religion, ethnicity or sexual orientation. In terms of the effects of disseminating hateful messages, there is today the added impact of the Internet. [Emphasis added.]
[58] In Keegstra, considering whether s. 319(2) minimally impaired the s. 2(b) freedom, the Chief Justice found that the offence was not overbroad in part because the section does not cover communications made in public places which are intended to be private. Presumably, although this question is not before this Court, the same would apply to communications on the Internet that were intended to be private. The prohibition is thus narrower than that covered by s. 319(1). At 773, the Chief Justice found it significant that criminalization of a communication does not depend on the form of the communication or the forum in which it is made:
Is s. 319(2) nevertheless overbroad because it captures all public expression intended to promote hatred? It would appear not, for the harm which the government seeks to prevent is not restricted to certain mediums and/or locations. To attempt to distinguish between various forms and fora would therefore be incongruent with Parliament's legitimate objective.
[Emphasis in original.]
[59] The conclusion in Keegstra that the harm which the legislation targets is not restricted to certain media or fora is an answer to the defence assertion that the use of the Internet for communication raises a new issue. The objective is to criminalize hate speech which is communicated other than in private conversation; and most of the statements communicated on the Internet are not in private conversation.
[60] Another problem with the defence argument is that it ignores the demanding mens rea requirement created by s. 319(2). At 774-75, Dickson C.J. concluded that "wilfully" promoting hatred means that the mental element of the offence is "satisfied only where an accused subjectively desires the promotion of hatred or foresees such a consequence as certain or substantially certain " At 776-77, the Chief Justice found that "the word "promotes" indicates active support or instigation. In "promotes" we thus have a word that indicates more than simple encouragement or advancement. The hate-monger must intend or foresee as substantially certain a direct and active stimulation of hatred against an identifiable group." In addition, at 777, he concluded that the term "hatred" as used in the section "connotes emotion of an intense and extreme nature that is clearly associated with vilification and detestation."
[61] The demanding mens rea requirement as expressed in Keegstra is a critical part of the proportionality test at the second stage of the Oakes analysis. The defence did not advance any argument to explain why or how someone who merely republishes or posts a link to hate literature could be found to satisfy the mens rea requirement of the offence. More importantly, it is evident that the proportionality analysis in Keegstra is readily applicable to internet communications.
[62] Lastly, the defence analogy to RJR-MacDonald does not hold. In RJR-MacDonald, the Court found that a provision banning cigarette advertising was not rationally connected to its objective because of the wide availability of imported tobacco advertising. The issue in Keegstra was quite different. First, the s. 319(2) offence is not targeted at the material per se, but the wilful promotion of hatred. In Whatcott at para. 51, the Court noted:
Hate speech legislation is not aimed at discouraging repugnant or offensive ideas. It does not, for example, prohibit expression which debates the merits of reducing the rights of vulnerable groups in society. It only restricts the use of expression exposing them to hatred as a part of that debate. It does not target the ideas, but their mode of expression in public and the effect that this mode of expression may have.
[63] RJR-MacDonald, by contrast, considered a law which targeted tobacco advertisements because the legislature considered that exposure to them was itself harmful. Given that the legislation effectively exempted 65% of admittedly harmful advertisements, it could not be rationally connected to the objective. The defence bases their argument on a misconception of the ambit of s. 319(2). Here, the harm does not arise from simple availability of the materials, but from wilfully using them to promote hatred. RJR-MacDonald did not change the relevant law.
[64] In summary, the fact that the Internet allows easy access to Mr. Topham's views and to the publications to which he provided links, does not raise a new legal issue sufficient to revisit the Chief Justice's careful analysis in Keegstra of the proportionality between the effects of the limiting measure and the objective of s. 319(2).
Whatcott changes the s. 1 analysis
[65] The defence asserts that Whatcott has changed the assessment of whether the means chosen in s. 319(2) are reasonable and demonstrably justified. The defence says that following Whatcott, the suppression of hate speech is only constitutional if the limitation on freedom of expression is rationally connected to some tangible form of harm. The defence expressed this argument in written submissions as follows:
Whatcott and Keegstra, when read in conjunction, suggest that hate speech laws are only constitutional in so far as the limitation on freedom of expression is rationally connected to some tangible harm or injury. Where the communication of hate propaganda is not rationally connected to any tangible harm, it follows that the application of section 319(2) is not rationally connected to any pressing and substantial objective.
The Cohen Committee relied entirely on correlational studies, based on subjective self-reports of harm, and did not contemplate the need to establish a causal relationship between hatred and harm, as discussed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Whatcott.
[66] The defence advances this argument because it says the modern behavioural approach to psychology raises a question about whether hate speech actually harms people. As discussed below, this argument must fail for a number of reasons. However, even assuming that Dr. Persinger's opinion is sufficient to raise an issue regarding the extent of psychological harm experienced by individuals exposed to hate speech, this argument fails to acknowledge the Chief Justice's detailed analysis explaining pressing and substantial harm caused by hate speech. The reasons highlight not only individual harm, but social harm and harm to the political process. Examples of the broader nature of the harm acknowledged by the Chief Justice include the repudiation of democratic values (at 765) and the relations between cultural and religious groups in Canadian society (at 767):
I am very reluctant to attach anything but the highest importance to expression relevant to political matters. But given the unparalleled vigour with which hate propaganda repudiates and undermines democratic values, and in particular its condemnation of the view that all citizens need be treated with equal respect and dignity so as to make participation in the political process meaningful, I am unable to see the protection of such expression as integral to the democratic ideal so central to the s. 2(b) rationale. (at 765)
Those who would uphold the provision argue that the criminal prohibition of hate propaganda obviously bears a rational connection to the legitimate Parliamentary objective of protecting target group members and fostering harmonious social relations in a community dedicated to equality and multiculturalism. I agree, for in my opinion it would be difficult to deny that the suppression of hate propaganda reduces the harm such expression does to individuals who belong to identifiable groups and to relations between various cultural and religious groups in Canadian society. (at 767)
[67] A similar argument to the one advanced by the defence here was rejected in Whatcott. The Court considered an argument that human rights legislation restricting hate speech was overbroad because it did not require proof of actual harm. Justice Rothstein, writing for the Court, reviewed the findings in Keegstra in order to explain the impacts of hate speech. He noted at para. 73 that Keegstra identified two types of pressing and substantial harm: the psychological and social consequences to individual members of the targeted group; and "the harmful effects on society at large by increasing discord and by affecting a subtle and unconscious alteration of views concerning the inferiority of the targeted group."
[68] When addressing the rational connection arm of the Oakes test at paras. 79-82, the Court further highlighted the importance of both the individual and social dimensions of the risk of harm posed by hate speech. In doing so, the Court emphasized the harm that flows to the group and society, concluding that:
[T]he focus must be on the likely effect of the hate speech on how individuals external to the group might reconsider the social standing of the group. Ultimately, it is the need to protect the societal standing of vulnerable groups that is the objective of legislation restricting hate speech.
[69] In Whatcott, the Court rejected the argument that a prohibition on hate speech required proof of actual harm. In doing so, the Court emphasized the particularly insidious nature of hate speech. It also acknowledged the difficulty in proving actual harm at para. 129:
In answer to the specific criticism that a prohibition against hate speech lacked a requirement to prove actual harm, Dickson C.J. argued in Keegstra (at p. 776) that both the difficulty of establishing a causal link between an expressive statement and the resulting hatred, and the seriousness of the harm to which vulnerable groups are exposed by hate speech, justifies the imposition of preventive measures that do not require proof of actual harm.
[70] The Court referred to Thomson Newspapers Co. v. Canada (Attorney General), [1998] 1 S.C.R. 877, for the proposition that a reasonable apprehension of harm test applies when the nature of the expression undermines the position of groups or individuals as equal participants in society since it is difficult or impossible to establish scientifically the type of harm. The Court concluded at para. 135:
As was clear from Taylor, and reaffirmed through the evidence submitted by interveners in this appeal, the discriminatory effects of hate speech are part of the everyday knowledge and experience of Canadians. I am of the opinion that the Saskatchewan legislature is entitled to a reasonable apprehension of societal harm as a result of hate speech.
[71] Contrary to the defence's submissions, Whatcott does not amount to a development of law that changes the s. 1 analysis in Keegstra, let alone a significant development in the law. Indeed, the Court in Whatcott affirmed the analysis undertaken in Keegstra and rejected the proposition that proof of harm is required.
Issue 2: Is there a change in circumstances or evidence that fundamentally shifts the parameters of the debate?
[72] The defence relies on the evidence of Dr. Persinger for the proposition that the parameters of the debate surrounding the s. 1 justification of s. 319(2) have fundamentally shifted. Dr. Persinger's December 7, 2006 report titled The Anachronism of Policies and Laws for Hate Speech in Modern Canada: The Current Negative Cultural Impact of Legal Punishment upon Extreme Verbal Behaviour was entered as an exhibit and he was cross-examined. The report was originally prepared for and tendered in evidence in Warman v. Lemire, 2009 CHRT 26 ("Lemire"), a hearing of a complaint under s. 13 of the Canadian Human Rights Act. At the hearing before the Canadian Human Rights Tribunal, the respondent relied on Dr. Persinger's opinion to challenge the constitutionality of that section.
[73] I admitted Dr. Persinger's report in evidence even though I had serious concerns regarding some of the opinions expressed in the report. In R. v. Topham (6 October 2016), Quesnel 25166 (B.C.S.C.), I ruled that he could give opinion evidence in the fields of cognitive psychology, clinical neuropsychology and behavioural neuroscience. With regard to the Crown argument, and my concern that Dr. Persinger's report was argumentative and contained opinions outside of his field of expertise, I found that it was difficult to rule on these issues without full cross-examination on the report. Accordingly, the report was admitted in evidence and I indicated I would rule on those issues, if necessary, when considering argument on the threshold issue.
[74] Having heard the cross-examination of Dr. Persinger and argument on the threshold issues, I conclude that the objections the Crown raised about the report are well founded. Dr. Persinger's report contains argument disguised as opinion and offers opinions well beyond his expertise including, as the Crown argues, at:
· page 1, paragraphs 1 and 2;
· page 2, 3rd full paragraph and 4th full paragraph;
· page 3, 1st, 4th and final paragraphs;
· page 4, full paragraphs 3-6 inclusive;
· page 5, full paragraphs 2-6 inclusive; and
· page 6, last paragraph.
[75] I do not intend to set out or analyze these passages or, indeed, other portions of the report given the fatal shortcoming of the defence argument that Dr. Persinger's opinion changes the parameters of the debate. To the extent that Dr. Persinger offers an opinion within his field of expertise, it is that there have been significant developments in the field of clinical neuropsychology and behavioural neuroscience since 1966 when the Cohen Committee report was published. This criticism was explained in Lemire at para. 235:
Dr. Persinger testified that Dr. Kaufmann's conclusions were out of date and based on inaccurate psychological theories. However, although Dr. Persinger referred to technological advances in neuroscience and neuropsychology that have enabled researchers to better understand how the brain works, most of his criticisms seemed to be directed to the methodology followed by Dr. Kaufmann in reaching his conclusions rather than on any scientific advances. In particular, Dr. Persinger criticized Dr. Kaufmann for having relied on correlational studies rather than direct experimental evidence
[76] Dr. Persinger repeated those critiques here. He emphasized that no experimental data was available in the 1960s because the powerful tool of brain imagining did not yet exist.
[77] The shortcoming of the defence position is that, to the extent that Dr. Persinger's evidence is admissible, it is insignificant and likely irrelevant to the debate about the s. 1 justification as expressed in Keegstra and developed in Whatcott. Earlier in these reasons, I set out a detailed review of the reasoning in those cases. That review is based on the argument advanced by the Crown, which provided an even more extensive review of those decisions. I adopt the following characterization of the s. 1 justification debate from the Crown's written submissions:
This extensive review of Keegstra and Whatcott establishes that the debate surrounding the s. 1 justification of s. 319(2) of the Code specifically - and hate speech generally - is rich and multifaceted. It involves wide ranging legal considerations that are informed by an amalgam of historical, psychological, sociological and philosophical concepts, along [with] common sense arising from human and judicial experience. While these disciplines contribute in various measures to the issues involved in the s. 1 analysis, the exercise remains fundamentally a task of weighing and balancing important and competing social and legal values.
[78] Dr. Persinger is unable to opine in the fields of history, social psychology, sociology, philosophy or political theory, all of which shaped the debate in Keegstra. His expertise in behavioural neuroscience was acknowledged to be quite distinct from social psychology. Accordingly, to the extent that he offered criticism of Dr. Kaufmann's study, this criticism must be accorded little or no weight. He has no expertise in social psychology and no familiarity with academic literature on hate speech. Indeed, he has never authored a report or published anything about hate speech. Further, he did not directly challenge the conclusions of Dr. Kaufmann or the Cohen Committee.
[79] His opinion offers nothing of significance to weighing and balancing the important, competing social and legal values analyzed in Keegstra. The evidence presented does not shift, let alone fundamentally, the parameters of that debate.
[80] As I indicated earlier, Dr. Persinger's opinion was originally submitted in evidence in Lemire. The question in that case was whether the opinion required the Tribunal to reconsider the ruling in Canada (Human Rights Commission) v. Taylor, [1990] 3 S.C.R. 892. Of course, Taylor was the companion decision to Keegstra and was released at the same time by the Court. It considered the constitutionality of the Canadian Human Rights Act. Lemire predates the decision in Bedford, but the Tribunal considered a similar question: whether there had been a sweeping change in the applicable science sufficient for the Tribunal to disregard the s. 1 analysis in Taylor. The Tribunal in Lemire concluded as follows at para. 236:
In my view, Dr. Persinger's criticism could just as easily have also been levelled in 1990 when Taylor was decided. This attempt to revisit or reconsider the Court's findings with respect to the Cohen Committee's report is not grounded in either of the subsequent amendments to the hate messaging provisions of the [Human Rights] Act, nor is the evidence persuasive that there has been some sort of sweeping change in the applicable science to warrant my disregarding the findings of Taylor with respect to the first component of the Oakes s. 1 analysis.
[81] I arrive at the same conclusion in this case. Dr. Persinger's evidence does not fundamentally shift the parameters of the debate for the s. 1 justification of s. 319(2) that was carried out in Keegstra. Indeed, given its narrow relevance and lack of substance, it does not begin to raise an issue that changes the parameters of the debate in a way that requires reconsideration.
Summary
[82] Mr. Topham has failed to identify a new legal issue about the s. 1 justification for s. 319(2) that was not considered in Keegstra. Further, he has failed to identify any circumstances or present any evidence which would fundamentally shift the parameters of the debate about the s. 1 justification. The defence application challenging the constitutionality of s. 319(2) is accordingly dismissed.
"Butler J."
